---
title: "Matching & Propensity Scores"
subtitle: "Causal inference under selection on observables"
---

## Overview

The quasi-experimental designs in the previous modules --- DiD, IV, RD --- all find some lever of as-good-as-random variation. But often no such lever exists, and we must lean on a different, stronger assumption: that we have *measured enough* to make treated and untreated units comparable. This is **selection on observables**, or conditional unconfoundedness.

Under this assumption, the job is to compare like with like. Matching pairs each treated unit with similar controls; propensity scores collapse a high-dimensional covariate set into a single comparability index; inverse-probability weighting reweights the sample into a balanced pseudo-population; and doubly robust estimators hedge against modeling mistakes. Crucially, the credibility of all of these rests on two checkable conditions --- **overlap** and **balance** --- and one uncheckable one: that no unobserved confounder remains.

This module develops these tools and, just as importantly, the diagnostics that tell you whether an observational comparison can be trusted at all.

## Pages in This Module

- [Selection on Observables](selection-on-observables.qmd) --- The conditional independence assumption and when adjustment works.
- [Matching Methods](matching-methods.qmd) --- Exact, nearest-neighbor, and caliper matching.
- [Propensity Scores](propensity-scores.qmd) --- The balancing score and how to use it.
- [Weighting and Doubly Robust Estimation](weighting-ipw.qmd) --- IPW, stabilized weights, and AIPW.
- [Overlap and Balance Diagnostics](overlap-diagnostics.qmd) --- Common support, standardized differences, and trimming.
